A photon can spend 100,000 years fighting its way out of the Sun's core, then cross to Earth in just over 8 minutes.
Time runs a little faster at your head than at your feet, because your head sits slightly farther from the mass of the Earth.
Trillions of neutrinos from the Sun stream through your body every second, and almost none ever touch a single atom of you.
Atoms are mostly empty space. Squeeze out the emptiness and the entire human race would fit into the volume of a sugar cube.
Helium cooled near absolute zero becomes a superfluid that flows without any friction and can creep up and over the walls of its container.
A single teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about a billion tons here on Earth.
Absolute zero can never be reached. Thermodynamics lets you get arbitrarily close but forbids ever arriving.
Entangled particles show matching results instantly across any distance, yet entanglement cannot carry a message faster than light.
Empty space is never truly empty. Virtual particles constantly appear and vanish even in a perfect vacuum.
Hold two metal plates close together in a vacuum and the vacuum itself pushes them together. That is the Casimir effect.
Hospitals use antimatter every day. PET scans work by detecting positrons annihilating inside your body.
Your body glows. Humans constantly emit infrared light, plus a faint visible glow about a thousand times too dim for eyes to see.
A lightning bolt heats the air around it to roughly 30,000 kelvin, about 5 times hotter than the surface of the Sun.
Glass does not flow. Old windows are thicker at the bottom because of how the panes were made, not because glass is a slow liquid.
Water is a rebel among substances: its solid floats on its liquid. Almost everything else sinks when it freezes.
The Sun shines by quantum cheating. Its protons lack the energy to fuse by classical rules, so they tunnel through the barrier instead.
Current flowing in a superconducting loop meets exactly zero resistance and can circulate for years without fading.
Muons made by cosmic rays should decay long before reaching the ground. Time dilation stretches their lives, and we detect them at sea level daily.
Over 99 percent of your mass does not come from the Higgs field. It comes from the energy binding quarks inside your protons and neutrons.
In the vacuum of space, two clean pieces of the same metal pressed together can fuse permanently. Engineers call it cold welding.
Earth's days are slowly getting longer. Tidal drag from the Moon slows our spin by about 2 milliseconds per century.
Every second, the Sun converts about 4 million tons of its own mass into pure energy.
Gravity is astonishingly weak. A cheap toy magnet lifting a paperclip out-pulls the gravity of the entire planet.
Particles can outrun light in water. When they do, they emit an eerie blue glow called Cherenkov radiation, visible in nuclear reactor pools.
Flames in orbit have no up. In free fall, a candle burns as a dim, ghostly blue sphere.
Temperature is just molecular motion. Nitrogen molecules in room-temperature air move faster than a passenger jet.
Black holes are not cosmic vacuum cleaners. Swap the Sun for a black hole of equal mass and Earth's orbit would not change at all.
Black holes evaporate. Through Hawking radiation, one with the Sun's mass would take about 10 to the power 67 years to vanish.
Fall toward a small black hole and tides stretch you lengthwise while squeezing you sideways. Physicists genuinely call it spaghettification.
Cross the event horizon of a giant black hole and you might notice nothing special at that moment. The point of no return has no sign.
The Sun's surface is about 5,500 degrees Celsius, yet its wispy outer atmosphere blazes at millions of degrees. Physicists still argue about why.
No two people ever see the same rainbow. Each one is built from light angles unique to a single observer's position.
Mirrors do not swap left and right. They flip front to back, and your brain interprets the rest.
Quantum theory predicts the electron's magnetism correctly to about 1 part in a trillion, the most precise test in all of science.
One glass of water contains more molecules than all the oceans on Earth contain glasses of water.
Statistically, each breath you inhale contains a few molecules exhaled in Julius Caesar's dying breath.
Perpetual motion machines are outlawed twice over: once by conservation of energy and again by the second law of thermodynamics.
Near absolute zero, thousands of atoms can merge into a Bose Einstein condensate and behave as one single giant quantum wave.
Sunlight physically pushes on things. Spacecraft have sailed the solar system on nothing but the gentle pressure of light on giant mirrors.
Fire single electrons one at a time at two slits and an interference pattern still builds up, as if each electron went through both.
Watch which slit an electron takes and the interference pattern disappears. The act of measurement changes the outcome.
Uncertainty is not sloppy measurement. Nature itself refuses to give a particle an exact position and an exact momentum at the same time.
Radioactive decay is genuinely random. Physics predicts exactly when half a sample will decay, but never which atom goes next.
On the summit of Everest, water boils at about 70 degrees Celsius, cool enough to ruin a proper cup of tea.
An Apollo astronaut dropped a hammer and a feather on the Moon. With no air, both hit the dust at exactly the same moment.
Einstein's Nobel Prize was not for relativity. He won it for explaining the photoelectric effect, a cornerstone of quantum theory.
The Hiroshima bomb converted less than a single gram of matter into energy. That is how potent E equals m c squared really is.
Look up and you look back in time. The Andromeda galaxy appears tonight as it actually was 2.5 million years ago.
Roughly 1 percent of the static on an old analog television was the afterglow of the Big Bang itself.
Protons might be immortal. None has ever been seen to decay, and experiments put their lifetime above 10 to the power 34 years.
As far as any experiment can measure, an electron has no size at all. It behaves as a perfect mathematical point.
Quarks are never found alone. Pull two apart and the energy you spend creates new quarks to keep every one of them company.
Outside a nucleus, a lone neutron survives only about 15 minutes on average before it decays.
Protons in the Large Hadron Collider circle its 27 kilometer ring more than 11,000 times every second.
By colliding lead nuclei, the Large Hadron Collider briefly creates matter trillions of degrees hot, the hottest stuff humans have ever made.
When matter meets antimatter, both convert entirely into energy. It is the most efficient reaction physics allows.
In the early universe, matter outnumbered antimatter by about one part in a billion. Everything you have ever seen is that tiny leftover.
Sound can make light. Collapse a tiny bubble in water with ultrasound and it flashes, briefly hotter than the surface of the Sun.
Crush a wintergreen candy in a dark room and it sparks with flashes of blue light. The effect is called triboluminescence.
Ice is slippery because of a liquid-like layer of molecules on its surface, not because your weight melts it, as textbooks long claimed.
A hot cup of coffee genuinely weighs more than the same cup cold. Heat is energy, and energy has mass, just immeasurably little of it.
Ordinary magnetism around a wire is relativity in disguise, an effect of moving electric charges and length contraction.
Earth's magnetic poles have traded places hundreds of times. During a flip, compasses would gradually swing around to point south.
A whip cracks because its tip breaks the sound barrier. That snap is a miniature sonic boom.
Ignoring air resistance, a bullet fired level and one dropped from the same height strike flat ground at the same instant.
Right now you are riding the galaxy at about 800,000 kilometers per hour. You feel nothing, because steady motion is undetectable.
Astronauts are not beyond gravity. The space station feels about 90 percent of surface gravity; the crew simply never stops falling.
When two black holes merged over a billion light years away, detectors on Earth measured a stretch far smaller than a single proton.
Starlight visibly bends around the Sun. Measuring that bend during a 1919 eclipse made Einstein world famous almost overnight.
Gravity on a neutron star is so fierce that its tallest mountains stand only millimeters high.
Atomic clocks are now so sensitive they can register time running faster after being lifted just a few centimeters higher.
The kilogram is no longer a metal cylinder in a Paris vault. Since 2019 it has been defined through Planck's constant.
Microwave ovens hit no special resonance of water. They simply shove polar molecules back and forth billions of times per second.
At one special combination of low pressure and temperature, water boils and freezes at the same time. It is called the triple point.
Water microwaved in a smooth cup can silently heat past its boiling point, then erupt the instant you disturb it.
Observe a quantum system often enough and you can freeze its evolution entirely. It is called the quantum Zeno effect.
Quantum teleportation is real, but it transfers a state rather than matter, and it still needs a slower-than-light signal to work.
Much of matter's solidity comes from the Pauli exclusion principle: electrons flatly refuse to pile into the same quantum state.
A white dwarf packs the Sun's mass into a ball the size of Earth. A spoonful of it would weigh several tons.
Gravity travels at light speed. Waves and light from a 2017 neutron star merger arrived within 2 seconds of each other after 130 million years.
The most distant galaxies recede from us faster than light. No law is broken; space itself is expanding and carries them along.
Everything ever seen by any telescope adds up to about 5 percent of the universe. The rest is dark matter and dark energy.
In 1991, detectors caught a single proton from space carrying the kinetic energy of a well-pitched baseball. It was nicknamed the Oh My God particle.
A static shock from a doorknob can top 20,000 volts. It stays harmless because almost no current actually flows.
The pressure wave from the 1883 Krakatoa eruption circled the entire planet at least 3 times, recorded on barometers worldwide.
Sound in air maxes out near 194 decibels. Push any harder and it stops being sound and becomes a shock wave.
Inside the quietest rooms on Earth, you can hear your own heartbeat and the blood moving through your ears.
The textbook story of wing lift, with air racing over the top to reunite underneath, is false. Wings work largely by pushing air downward.
Information is physical. Erasing even a single bit of data must release a small amount of heat, a rule known as Landauer's principle.
Scientists once levitated a living frog with a powerful magnet, because water is weakly diamagnetic. The feat won an Ig Nobel Prize.
Pitch shatters like a solid when struck, yet it flows. One famous experiment has caught only 9 falling drops since 1927.
Electric signals travel through wires at near light speed, but the electrons themselves drift along slower than a crawling snail.
In alternating current, the electrons never really go anywhere. They just wiggle back and forth, about 50 or 60 times a second.
Cosmic rays occasionally flip bits in computer memory. Some mysterious crashes really are caused by particles from space.
Many smoke detectors hold a tiny speck of americium, a radioactive element that does not occur in nature.
About 2 billion years ago in what is now Gabon, uranium deposits ran as natural nuclear reactors for hundreds of thousands of years.
Nobody measures the speed of light anymore. Its value is now exact by definition, and the meter itself is defined from it.
Every measurement of light's speed ever made is a round trip. The one-way speed by itself has never been directly measured.
Whether two distant events happen at the same time depends on how you are moving. The universe has no single now.
Travel fast enough for long enough and you return younger than your twin. Atomic clocks flown on airliners have confirmed the effect.
Mercury's orbit drifts in a way Newton's gravity cannot explain. Einstein's general relativity accounts for it precisely.
Neutrinos shape-shift between three identities as they travel. That discovery proved they have mass and earned a Nobel Prize.
Antimatter was predicted by pure mathematics. Dirac's equation demanded it in 1928, and the positron appeared in a detector in 1932.
Helium was discovered in sunlight before anyone found it on Earth, spotted as a strange line in the Sun's spectrum in 1868.
Drop water onto a pan far hotter than boiling and it skates around on a cushion of its own steam, outlasting water on a cooler pan.
Mix cornstarch with water and you can briefly run across a pool of it. The fluid turns rigid under sudden force.
